introduction: This refers to extensive necrosis of the intestine of multi-factorial origin. It may ultimately result in intestinal perforation. It commonly affects the terminal ileum and proximal colon but it may involve the entire length of the gut. Predisposing factors include: Prematurity and very low birth weight, early infant formula feeding, intra-uterine growth restriction, polycythaemia, septicaemia, umbilical catheterization, congenital heart diseases.

features: plain abdominal X-Ray shows gaseous bowel distension or presence of gas within the bowel wall (pneumatosis intestinalis)
Abdominal tenderness and rigidity (evidence of perforation)

non_drug: Insert a nasogastric tube to decompress the stomach and for regular aspiration
Serial plain abdominal X-Ray (supine and right lateral)
Correct thrombocytopaenia and deranged coagulation profile with appropriate blood product
Manage shock using crystalloids or colloids and inotropes
Monitor urinary output for anti-shock therapy success
drug: Antibiotics are administered intravenously: Cephalosporin (ceftriaxone, cefotaxime or ceftazidime 100 mg/kg/day)

definitive_treatment: Continue antibiotics and nil per os for 7 – 10 days if diagnosis is confirmed with X-Ray findings
Re-introduce gradual oral feeds after 48 hours if diagnosis remains unconfirmed and baby recovers quickly, while antibiotic therapy continues for 5 days
Surgery is indicated by clinical deterioration or intestinal perforation
